w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh wp14

w:r.w:t: HAWASSA UNIVERSITY INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY FACULTY OF INFORMATICS
A PROJECT SUBMITTED IN PARTIAL FULFILLMENT OF THE REQUIREMENT FOR THE AWARD OF
We declare that this project has been composed

solely by ourselves and that it has not been submitted, in whole or in part, in any previous application for a degree. Except where stated otherwise by reference or acknowledgement, the work presented is entirely our own. Group members name and signature:
First and foremost, we would like to praise the Almighty God for giving us the strength, health, and endurance to complete this project successfully.
We would like to express our deepest gratitude to our advisor,
for his invaluable guidance, constructive criticism, and patience throughout the development of the
system. His insights helped steer this project in the right direction and ensured we met our academic requirements.
We also wish to extend our appreciation to all the participants who took the time to respond to our questionnaires and participate in the interviews.
We are extremely grateful to our families for their unceasing prayer, financial support
would like to thank our friends and classmates who offered suggestions, feedback, and encouragement during the research and development phases of this project.
(Rotating Savings and Credit Association) serves as a vital traditional financial instrument for saving and social bonding. However, the traditional manual execution of
is often plagued by challenges such as lack of transparency, geographical limitations, mis

management of funds, and disputes regarding the lottery process. These issues are particularly pronounced in a dynamic university environment where students and staff have tight schedules and limited physical interaction time for such financial gatherings.
The primary objective of this project,
, is to design and develop a web-based management system that digitizes the traditional
process tailored specifically for the university community. The system aims to automate the core functionalities of
, including group creation, member registration, contribution tracking, and the lottery selection process.

chieve this, the project utilized a structured system development methodology. Requirements were gathered through interviews and questionnaires distributed among university students and staff to understand the specific needs and pain points of the target u

sers. Object-Oriented Analysis and Design (OOAD) techniques, including Use Case and Sequence diagrams, were employed to model the system's architecture. The application was developed using modern web technologies to ensure responsiveness and accessibility.
The resulting system successfully provides a secure and transparent platform where users can join
groups, pay contributions digitally, and view real-time status updates. The automated lottery algorithm ensures fairness, eliminating human bias. Consequently,
not only modernizes a cultural financial practice but also enhances financial inclusion and trust within the university community.
CHAPTER TWO: DESCRIPTIONS OF EXISTING SYSTEM
4.2 Purpose of the System Design Document
ETB: Ethiopian Birr (referenced in the cost estimation).
ID: Identification (used for student identity numbers).
IDE: Integrated Development Environment (referencing tools like Visual Studio Code).
APK: Android Package (the file format for Android application distribution).
USSD: Unstructured Supplementary Service Data (used for simulating payment workflows).
The management of personal finances presents a significant challenge for university students worldwide. Often operating with limited budgets from part-time work, family support, or

student loans, students must balance essential living expenses, educational materials, and social activities. This financial strain is particularly acute in developing economies, where economic instability and inflation can rapidly erode purchasing power.
system has long served as an effective community-based financial practice that encourages saving, trust, and mutual support.
is a rotating savings and credit association in which members contribute a fixed amount of money within a s

pecified period, and the total sum is awarded to one member in each cycle. This system promotes cooperation, discipline, and a sense of financial responsibility among participants. Despite its long-standing success and cultural importance, the traditional
method is primarily manual and face-to-face, which limits its convenience and efficiency, especially in modern urban and academic settings.
aligns with Ethiopia’s ongoing efforts toward digital transformation and financial inclusion. By combining cultural traditions with modern technological solutions,
not only preserves the essence of the

practice but also adapts it to meet contemporary financial and social needs. Ultimately, this initiative contributes to the empowerment of students, enabling them to manage their finances more effectively and to develop
responsible saving habits that will benefit them beyond their academic years.
University students often face financial difficulties due to limited and inconsistent sources

of income, inadequate budgeting skills, and a lack of structured saving mechanisms. These financial challenges can negatively affect their academic performance, and overall quality of life. University students face a persistent and multifaceted problem of
and a lack of accessible savings mechanisms. The existing financial infrastructure often fails to meet their specific needs, resulting in several critical issues:
Many students find formal savings accounts cumbersome due to
, and bureaucratic hurdles. Loan facilities are typically inaccessible due to a lack of credit history or collateral.

ructured and motivating system, students struggle to cultivate consistent saving habits. Their limited income is often consumed by immediate expenses, leaving little to no room for future planning, emergency funds, or investment in their educational needs.

are popular, they are not without drawbacks. They are logistically challenging to organize, vulnerable to mismanagement or default, and require physical presence for collection and distribution, which is impractical for a dispersed student body.
There is a significant gap in the market for financial technology (
) solutions that are tailored to the socio-economic context and cultural norms of Ethiopian students.

al problem is the absence of a secure, convenient, and culturally resonant financial tool that empowers university students to save money consistently, access lump-sum funds when needed, and build a foundation for long-term financial literacy and stability
To accomplish the general objective of the project, the following specific objectives are defined as sequential subtasks:
system and current financial saving practices among university students through interviews and questionnaires
the key problems and limitations of the current manual system, such as lack of transparency, geographical barriers, and mismanagement of funds
user requirements and system needs to ensure the platform is tailored to the socio-economic context of the campus community
system architecture, secure database schema, and an intuitive mobile user interface using React Native and
platform based on the design specifications to automate group creation and contribution tracking
the system for functionality, lottery fairness, usability, and performance using a series of unit, integration, and system tests
the system in the real environment through a phased approach, starting with internal alpha testing and moving to user acceptance testing (UAT)
a plan for future maintenance and potential enhancements, such as integration with live payment gateways like Chapa
The scope of this study encompasses designing, developing, and evaluating a mobile-based application known as

iversity students to facilitate digital savings and group contribution management within the university environment. This study covers both the technical aspects of the system implementation and how the system is put into practice in enhancing traditional
The proposed system will be developed as a mobile application that will target the Android mobile platform. This is because most students in the campus environment are using Android smartphones. The
mobile app allows the users to create, manage, and participate in
This study will be geographically and functionally limited to one university environment where the test and evaluation of the application would be conducte

d. While this current scope of work focuses on the university level, the design and architecture shall be flexible in a way that would allow its future scalability, such as adding cross-campus functionality, real payment integrations, or web-based support.
project aims to provide a reliable and user-friendly financial platform, the study is subject to the following limitations:
The system will be limited to campus-based users and will not extend to the general public.
The application will initially support only Android devices, limiting access for web users.
Internet dependency may restrict usage in areas with poor network connectivity.
is far more than a savings application; it serves as a transformative tool that was designed to meet the unique financial challenges that university students face. By digitizing the conventional "
" system, it offers value across many dimensions to its unique user groupings:
is a fundamental tool for financial empowerment and crisis management, from which students can begin to manage their finances formally and intuitively. It helps students build

steady savings habits and financial discipline since they operate within something very familiar. Thus, it directly addresses the most pressing needs of students through offering a reliable mechanism to liquidate a sum of money during emergencies, such as

a broken laptop or medical costs, or for major purchases like textbooks, without recourse to high-interest loans. Such smoothing of cash flow reduces a considerable source of stress, enabling students to focus more effectively on their academic pursuits.
acts as a powerful management tool that simplifies how
groups operate. It automates the entire process —

from member registration to contribution tracking — and reduces the burden of manual coordination. Organizers no longer have to worry about cash handling, recording contributions, or following up on delayed payments. The system automatically keeps transpar

ent records, sends reminders, and determines whose turn it is to receive the pooled fund. This automation helps organizers save time, maintain fairness, and focus more on group engagement and growth rather than dealing with repetitive administrative tasks.
provides a robust, data-driven framework designed to manage complex peer-to-peer financial cycles with minimal manual overhead. The system centra

lizes administrative oversight through automated transaction logs, real-time status monitoring, and system-wide audit trails. By automating the lottery logic and payment verification, the platform reduces the risk of human error and administrative fatigue.
At the systemic and community levels,
serves as a catalyst for digital inclusion and social cohesion. The project modernizes a trusted cultural tradition, connects heritage with innovation, and opens routes

to a cashless campus ecosystem. It reinforces the community bonds through building digital trust networks with peers and promotes a culture of mutual support and collective responsibility. Furthermore, the project positions itself as a scalable homegrown

solution contributing to the local digital economy and showing how technology can be used to solve particular community challenges, thus empowering a new generation to engage with financial technology with confidence.

in a way that reflects the lived practices, constraints, and aspirations of university students, we will undertake a rigorous, mixed-methods data collection program. This program combines qualitative dept

h with quantitative breadth to ensure that product decisions (features, flows, notifications, and trust mechanisms) are empirically grounded. We will implement the plan during the first eight weeks of the project (aligned with the Planning & Design phase).
addresses real financial challenges faced by students, multiple data collection methods were employed:
to understand how they currently manage savings and group contributions (
Distributed through Google Forms to gather quantitative data on saving habits, preferred contribution intervals, and mobile app usage comfort from students.
For this project, a hybrid approach will be adopted, utilizing
principles for project management and an
methodology for system analysis and design.

The Agile approach is chosen for its flexibility and iterative nature. As a mobile app project with a small team, this allows us to adapt to changing requirements and get user feedback (from our team and test users) in short cycles.
The OO approach is selected for analysis and design, as it focuses on combining data and processes into objects. This is ideal for a system like
, which will manage real-world entities such as 'Users', '
Groups', and 'Contributions'. This approach will allow us to build a modular, scalable, and maintainable system using UML diagrams to model the system before implementation.
The software development environment includes the hardware and software tools used to build the
The implementation (coding) phase will follow an
paradigm. This approach is based on the concept of "objects" which may contain both data (attributes) and code (methods).
By using React Native (which is component-based and naturally aligns with OO principles) and
, the application will be designed as a collection of interacting objects (e.g.,

) that model the real-world domain. This method is chosen over procedural programming as it directly supports the Object-Oriented design from the previous section, leading to more modular, reusable, and maintainable code.
The testing and deployment methodology is designed to ensure the quality, reliability, and usability of the
Our testing strategy will investigate and provide information about the quality of the software by incorporating recognized levels of tests:
Individual components and functions will be tested in isolation.
Tested units will be combined and tested to ensure they
This will focus on verifying the "glue" between different parts of the app, such as navigation between screens.
The entire application will be tested from an end-user perspective to verify it meets all functional requirements.
(B) If the sandbox is inaccessible, a
will be developed to simulate the payment workflow. A
of users, groups, and transactions will be created to ensure the system can be tested thoroughly.
The deployment process will follow a phased approach:
The application will first be deployed and tested on virtual devices using Android Studio emulators.
(Android Package) file will be generated and distributed to the four team members for installation and testing on physical devices.
will be shared with a small, select group of target users.
file will be submitted as the final project deliverable.
because user financial information is involved. By using these approaches below, protection, integrity, and privacy of data are all ensured:
Each user makes a personalized account that gets checked with their university email or ID. Process includes:
Secure Login System: User passwords go through new adaptive hashing methods such as
Two-Factor Authentication (2FA): An OTP, meaning a One-Time Password, will be emailed or texted to the person for main actions like withdrawing or starting any group.
Role-Based Access Control (RBAC): Every user has special permissions, for example admin for a group or just a member.
The database will be set up as follows:
Admins with restricted access (project admins only change database schema).
Routine patching and updates are done for the database.
Foreign key constraint applies to verify data integrity and helps avoid unauthorized
HTTPS Protocol: The whole communication between app and server gets carried out in HTTPS, so interception is prevented.
Token-Based Authentication: Tokens are used to secure user session, minimizing chance of hijack.
To ensure that all user data, app settings, and financial records in
are securely backed up and can be quickly restored in case of accidental deletion, hardware failure, or system errors.
All critical data (user profiles, transactions, and app settings) will be automatically backed up to the cloud daily. The cloud provider (e.g.,
or Firebase) offers versioned backups to allow restoration to previous states.
transactions are temporarily stored on the user’s device in encrypted form, allowing recovery if network issues occur before cloud sync.
Backups are stored in multiple regions (where supported by the cloud provider) to prevent data loss from localized outages.
Users can restore their account data from the latest cloud
through the app’s settings if they switch devices or reinstall the app.

In case of server failure, the system administrator can restore the database from the most recent cloud backup. The recovery process is tested quarterly to ensure minimal downtime and integrity of restored data.
All backup data is encrypted in transit and at rest using industry-standard encryption
(e.g., AES-256).Access to backup systems is restricted to authorized personnel only.
Regular audits are conducted to ensure backup integrity and compliance with data protection standards.
Daily automatic backups for all critical user and app data.
Weekly full backups for system configurations and less frequently changed data.
Quarterly disaster recovery tests to validate restoration procedures.
This section outlines the system requirements for end-users who will install and use the
mobile application. It specifies the minimum and recommended hardware and software specifications necessary for smooth performance.
To install and store app data
Required for online transactions and synchronization
Ensures reliable group synchronization and notifications
A feasibility study is conducted to determine the practicality of the proposed system
mobile application, five key areas of feasibility are examined:
will utilize a popular cross-platform framework react Native to build the mobile app, and a robust backend technology with a cloud-hosted database (
). All chosen technologies are open-source, mature, and supported by extensive community documentation.
The development environment requires standard computing resources (laptops/desktops) and common IDEs (VS Code/Android Studio) that are readily available to the development team.
The team possesses the necessary skills in modern programming languages (JavaScript) and architecture design (client-server model) to successfully execute the project.
So according to the above stated points the proposed system is technically feasible. It relies on established, current, and accessible technologies well-suited for a modern mobile application.
The app directly solves a clear problem: the tedious, manual, and often opaque nature of traditional cash-based
systems. It streamlines group formation, contribution tracking, and notification processes.

Given the target audience (tech-savvy university students), the adoption of a mobile-based solution for a familiar financial mechanism is expected to be high, provided the interface is intuitive and secure.
The system is a standalone mobile application and backend, requiring no complex integration with
IT systems or external financial institutions (as per the scope).
From the above standpoints the proposed system is operationally feasible and, upon deployment, will deliver tangible benefits to the target community.
The core development tools (IDE, programming languages, SDKs) are free and open-source.
will utilize free-tier or low-cost cloud hosting services (e.g., AWS Free Tier, Firebase, or
) for the backend and database, which is sufficient for the limited scope and user load of an academic MVP
As a student project, the primary development cost is zero. The
economic investment is the developer’s time and effort
Thus, the project is economically feasible as it leverages open-source tools and academic resources, keeping costs minimal.
"Detailed requirements gathering, UX/UI mockups, and database design."
"Unit testing, integration testing, user acceptance testing (UAT) with a small sample group, and bug fixing."
"Finalizing the academic report and preparing the app for testing on a closed distribution platform (e.g., Firebase App Distribution)."
This provides a controlled buffer for unforeseen technical challenges.
Thus, the project is schedule feasible within the standard timeline allocated for a senior year project.
Since the app will not handle real-time cash transactions or integrate with banks, it avoids the complex regulatory burden of becoming a licensed financial service provider (

The design will adhere to high ethical standards regarding data privacy and security for all user profile and simulated financial data. Users will be fully informed that the platform is an academic simulation and not a registered financial service.
is legally and ethically feasible within the defined academic scope, though caution is necessary for future expansion.
Internet and direct phone calls for group meetings

We present a week-by-week schedule covering 34 weeks. The schedule subdivides phases into milestones, activities, dependencies, and acceptance criteria. Below we enumerate the major activities in each phase with deliverables and milestone checks.
CHAPTER TWO: DESCRIPTIONS OF EXISTING SYSTEM
In the current environment, students at the university who wish to save or participate in group-based financial activities such as
have to rely on traditional, manual methods. An
is a source of informal savings association that helps individuals develop financial discipline and access much-needed pooled resources. Despite being culturally important, the
system that students currently use has not been modernized and remains highly paper-based and informal.

groups with their classmates, roommates, or close friends. One person typically serves as the collector and record keeper for the management process, which is handled manually. A handwritten note, verbal confirmation, or messaging apps like Telegram and

d for contribution tracking. Although these techniques are somewhat successful, they are not sustainable approaches to money management. Missed contributions, inaccurate recording, and payment delays are the outcomes of an unstructured or automated system.

Lack of accountability and transparency are two more significant issues with this system. Participants must rely on informal agreements and trust because there is no central location for data storage. It become

s challenging to resolve misunderstandings or conflicts that occur, such as when a member questions the accuracy of records or someone forgets to pay. The lack of digital records that can be verified raises the risk of disagreements and may even cause the
group as a whole to fall apart. Students, who might not have a lot of experience with financial systems, are particularly prone to this issue.

he present system is that it is geographically bound. In order for a joint savings group to be formed, students need to be practically in close vicinity with one another. Many students who are living in different dorms cannot easily start or be part of an
group. This excludes a lot of students who could otherwise benefit from saving collectively since the process is heavily dependent on physical meetings and personal interaction.
Furthermore, there is no technological integration in the current system. Despite the fact that almost all students own mobile
no specialized digital platform has been created to effectively oversee the
activities. Students are not yet aware of the main advantages of digital solutions, such as transaction history tr

acking, digital wallet security, automated payables reminders, and performance analytics. Relying too much on these more manual approaches not only reduces convenience but also denies students the skills they need to manage their finances in a digital age.

ight also resist this change organizationally due to comfort with acquired habits or fear of adapting to new technology. Others may view digital financial systems as a threat to privacy or distrust them. For this reason, even when students realize the weak

nesses of the manual approach, most are very reluctant to explore innovative solutions. However, such resistance contributes to the perpetuation of inefficiency and lowers the potential impact that could be achieved with savings initiatives among students.
All things considered, university students manage

savings through a manual, unstructured, and ineffective system. The current system is very vulnerable to human error and potential conflict because it depends on verbal communication, a high degree of trust, and manual record-keeping. Transparency, au

tomation of contributions, and participation across campuses are not provided by any digital mechanism. Furthermore, students frequently don't know about alternative strategies or technological advancements that could enhance their experience saving money.
, especially in a university or student community context, has several notable strengths that contribute to its continuous practice and popularity. Although the traditional
operates manually without digital tools, it still serves as an effective and reliable informal financial system. This section discusses the major strengths of the current
system as observed among university students and community groups.
One of the strongest aspects of the existing
system is its simplicity. The concept and operation are easy to understand, even for individuals who have limited knowledg

e of finance or technology. Each member contributes a fixed amount of money at regular intervals, and the collected sum is given to one participant in each round. This simple cycle continues until every member has received their share. Because the process

is straightforward, members can easily participate without needing special training or complicated recordkeeping. This simplicity makes it accessible to all types of users, including students who may not have prior experience with formal financial systems.
Another major strength of the existing
system is the high level of social trust and cooperation among its members. Typically,

groups are formed among friends, classmates, or individuals who share a strong social bond. This personal connection promotes honesty, transparency, and accountability. Members rely on mutual trust rather than legal contracts, which

conflict and increases cooperation. In a university setting, this social trust helps students support one another financially, especially in times of need such as covering tuition fees, purchasing study materials, or managing living expenses.

system provides financial access to individuals who may not qualify for formal banking services. Many students or low-income individuals may not have access to credit, savings accounts, or loans from banks. However, through

, they can save money collectively and receive a lump-sum amount when their turn arrives. This encourages saving habits, supports emergency needs, and promotes a culture of mutual financial assistance. In this way,
plays a crucial role in promoting financial inclusion within the student community.
is also highly flexible. Members can determine the amount of contribution, the time interval (daily, weekly, or monthly), and the order in which members receive their turns. This flexibility allow

s groups to customize the system based on their financial capacity and needs. For university students, who often face irregular income or allowance schedules, this adaptability is a key advantage. The existing manual system can easily adjust to the members
circumstances without the need for strict formal procedures.

system strengthens social relationships among participants. Regular meetings and contributions create opportunities for members to interact, share experiences, and offer emotional as well as financial support. This social int

eraction builds unity and teamwork among university students, helping them develop stronger social networks. Such community support is important for students living away from their families, as it creates a sense of belonging and collective responsibility.
Another important strength of the existing
system is its low operational cost. Since it does not require technological tools, software, or external management, the e

xpenses are minimal. The only requirement is for members to keep a simple record of payments and turns. This makes it a cost-effective method for saving and lending money, especially for small groups of students or community members with limited resources.
system also promotes transparency and fairness. All members are usually present when contributions and payments are made, which ensu

res openness in financial transactions. Decisions such as rotation order, penalties for late payments, or membership changes are made collectively. This participatory decision-making process encourages accountability and a sense of ownership among members.
8. Motivation for Saving and Financial Discipline
encourages members to save regularly. Because

contributions are compulsory and consistent, members develop a habit of financial discipline. This is especially beneficial for students who are learning to manage their own finances. The system indirectly teaches budgeting, commitment, and responsibility
skills that are valuable in real-life financial management.
system, though traditional and manual, has
that contribute to its success and sustainability. Its simplicity, trust-based structure, low cost, and social nature make it an effective financial t

ool among university students. While it may lack technological efficiency, the human and social factors that define it—trust, cooperation, and community—remain powerful and relevant. These strengths form the foundation upon which an improved, computerized
System can be developed to maintain its traditional values while enhancing accessibility, accuracy, and efficiency through modern technology.

system has been working for quite a long time, there are still several challenges facing this system, especially when it is managed manually among university students. Observing from users and gathering feedback showed the following weaknesses:

The current system does almost everything manually: recording members, collecting contributions, and noting down payments. This makes the process very slow and difficult to manage when the number of participants increases.

Since it's a manual procedure for calculations and records, small mistakes are easily made that affect the whole contribution or payout process. Errors in dates, amounts, and names are common causes of confusion and complaints.
groups use paper notebooks and informal listings to record information; such records are easily lost, damaged, or forgotten when the person keeping the record is no longer available.

In this case, usually only the organizer has access to full details about the contributions and payouts. Members are expected to operate on a basis of trust, which might cause disagreements or suspicions in case something goes wrong.

They mostly notify their members of meetings, dates of collection, or results of draws through social media or by word of mouth. This is very informal and therefore some members may miss such information.

ent system relies on physical meetings, and that is sometimes a problem for busy members, those living off campus, or especially those who cannot attend regularly. At times, these factors delay contributions, adversely impacting the progress of the group.
Financial and personal information is stored without any protection. Any
person with access to the records can
easily view or misuse sensitive data.

The manual system cannot present any means through which trends can be established, reports generated, or analyses made on how well the group is performing. Every decision is made based on guesswork rather than data.
Generally speaking, the current system of

time-consuming and subject to human error, which also lacks transparency. This makes it difficult for members to stay updated and have trust in the process. The challenges piling up thus point to the real need for a modern, automated solution such as the
mobile app, which could make the whole system faster, clearer, and
tries to solve main challenges happening with normal

t most university students use. Today, with everything being done by hand, it is difficult for the people. Many problems happen, like losing records, not having enough information visible for everyone and troubles in organizing the group. Because of this,
is being made to put all these activities into a digital and easier approach. With
, instead of always managing on paper, there is now a mobile application which helps people handle the
in a quicker, clearer and safer way.
by creating and checking on their group through phones. This application let

s users sign themselves up, make new groups, bring in friends, plus decide the money and how often to pay. Every transfer of cash is put in the system’s storage automatically. This makes human error and confusion not as common as with hand-written records.
Instead of having things put on random pieces of paper or shared using social networks,

puts all information of the group into one joint database. It is simple for members to go and look at their payment histories, future chances to collect and the way their finances are progressing with the application. This clear look at the
builds the trust of members and makes certain that information is not kept away from some people in the group.
groups, the system also removes several earlier weaknesses. Now, reminders plus notices will come directly from the app, a

nd no person needs to make separate calls or try to remember to inform somebody. Through notifications, people will always remember their payment turn, future events or meetings. It is basically impossible to miss any important thing with automatic alerts.
Security and keeping data safe is an area that the system thinks is very important. Every student requires

in using their university ID or an email. Only true students are able to join the groups inside the app. For protection, all transaction and user records will be locked and saved in ways that unauthorized people cannot look at it.
Even if the system suddenly fails or removes some information, files can be brought back from special cloud backups.
finds solutions for almost all big issues that usually trouble the traditional
approach by making things more dependable,

giving transparency, and being easier to use. It keeps students from needing regular face-to-face meetings, avoids losing important information, and saves time. Also, it encourages the habit of saving and supports teamwork in this organized digital method.
Maybe for later versions, there is a possibility to connect the app directly with local payment tools like Chapa or
. This would make money transfer very dynamic. It might also connect users from other universities, not just one campus.
, even a web version could be made so more people can access it without phones.
is a new solution which mixes technology with the traditional
By doing this, students use their money better, make financial discipline stronger, and help the campus to have a group that pays more attention to money matters.
Paper-based notebooks; prone to loss or damage
Requires physical meetings for collection and lottery
Location-independent; students can participate from any dorm or campus
Ledger visible only to the organizer/secretary
Real-time Dashboard visible to all members showing who paid and who won
Manual drawing of names; prone to bias or manipulation
Automated Random Number Generator (RNG); auditable and fair
Verbal or informal chat messages; easy to forget
Cash is carried physically; high risk of theft/loss
Digital Transactions; funds are tracked securely via virtual accounts
system is a digital, mobile-first financial platform that gives college students easy-to-use, organized, and community-focused savings tools. It uses the traditional "

" (rotating savings and credit association) model in a new way to help students with their unique financial problems, like having irregular income, sharing expenses, and needing to save for short-term goals. This chapter talk

s about the basic features and quality standards that the system needs to meet in order to provide a safe, easy-to-use, and useful service. The features are designed with a student-centric approach, prioritizing simplicity, trust, and financial inclusion.
Functional requirements describe the core operations and behaviors of the UNI-
The system shall allow new users to create an account by entering their full name, phone number, and password.
The system must validate user inputs for format and uniqueness (specifically checking that the phone number is not already registered).
The system shall allow registered users to log in using their phone number and password.
The system must provide a mechanism for password recovery (e.g., via SMS verification).
The system shall temporarily lock a user account after multiple failed login attempts.
The system shall allow Members to submit a request to create a new
group by defining the proposed contribution amount, duration, and rules.
The system shall allow Admins to view pending
creation requests and approve or reject them based on platform policies.
The system shall allow Admins to suspend or terminate active
groups that violate platform rules, without directly intervening in the financial transaction flow.
The system shall allow members to browse active
and join a group if there are available slots and they meet eligibility criteria.
The system must check slot availability in real-time to prevent over-subscription.
Members shall be able to view the current status of their
The Treasurer (or Admin) shall be able to update settings, pause, or close
cycles, provided the changes do not conflict with active cycles.
The system must allow members to contribute payments via integrated payment gateways (mobile money, bank transfer, or card).
The system shall record successful payments and notify the member immediately.
The system shall allow members to view their paginated transaction history, filterable by date range and
The system shall allow the designated winner to request a withdrawal to a linked bank or mobile money account.
The system must validate the winner's identity and payout details before processing a withdrawal.
The system shall execute a random selection algorithm (RNG) to determine the winner of a cycle.
The system must verify that all contributions are closed and reconciled before a draw can be initiated.
The system shall automatically record the winner and notify all participants of the result.
The system shall allow Admins to verify student IDs and manage user account statuses.
The system shall allow Admins to view group activity logs for safety audits.
The Admin shall have the power to freeze groups flagged for suspicious activity, but
manually withdraw or transfer group funds.
The system shall generate financial and operational reports (aggregating transactions and membership data) in PDF or CSV formats.
The system shall send automated reminders (SMS/Push) to members for upcoming contributions based on the cycle schedule.
These requirements define the quality attributes, constraints, and performance expectations of the system.
Transaction records must remain immutable after confirmation to ensure financial accuracy.
The system must integrate with payment gateways using PCI-compliant standards for cards and secure APIs for mobile money.
The system must use a seeded Random Number Generator (RNG) for the lottery process and log the seed and results for audit purposes.
All administrative actions (member changes, rule edits) must be logged in an audit trail.
The system must support Know Your Customer (KYC) checks before allowing payout withdrawals.
The system should be functional on low-bandwidth networks to accommodate users with poor internet connections.
The system must handle concurrent edits and real-time joining requests without data conflicts.
The system should provide a local cache to allow users to view their status even when offline.
The system must include retry logic and
policies for failed notifications or third-party service glitches.
The system must provide clear, corrective feedback to users when invalid information is entered (e.g., during registration or payment).
The system must provide specific guidance/empty states when no data is available (e.g., empty payment history).
must consist of fixed equal contribution amounts and
The system shall enforce that a phone number can only be associated with a single user account.

This section introduces the proposed system with UML system models. System models describe the model of the envisioned system. For the new system model we will use case models, sequence diagrams, Analysis level class model, and state chart diagrams.
Use case identifies the actors involved in an interaction and names the type of interactions. This is then supplemented by additional informati

on describing the interaction with the system. The set of use cases represents all of the possible interactions that will be described in the system requirements. Actors in the process, who may be human or other systems, are represented as stick figures.
The following use cases diagram illustrates the major actors and system use case for
Allows a student to create a new identity on the platform by providing personal and academic details. This is the entry point for all financial activities
User opens the app and selects "Register".
The user opens the application and selects the
validates the input format (e.g., phone number length, password complexity).
checks the database to ensure the Phone Number or University ID is not already registered.
generates a One-Time Password (OTP) and sends it to the user's phone for verification.
verifies the OTP and creates a new user profile with a "Verified" or "Unverified" (pending KYC) status.
redirects the user to the Login screen.
Alternative Flow A: Invalid Input Data:
detects a phone number that does not match the required length or a password that fails security complexity rules
displays a specific error message and prompts the user to correct the fields
Alternative Flow B: Account Already Registered:
is already associated with an existing account
notifies the user and redirects them to the Login or "Forgot Password" screen.
A new user account is successfully created and stored in the database
Allows a registered user to authenticate and access their dashboard.
The user enters their credentials on the login screen and clicks "Login"
The user must have a registered and active account.
hashes the entered password and compares it with the stored hash in the database.
validates the user's current status (ensuring the account is not banned or suspended).
generates a secure session token to maintain the user's logged-in state.
determines that the password hash does not match the stored record.
displays an "Invalid Username or Password" error
checks the user status and finds the account is "Banned" or "Suspended" due to bad behavior
prevents access and displays a contact message for administration
The user is authenticated and granted access to member-restricted features
A Member submits a request to form a new
group, which remains inactive until an Admin reviews and approves it for safety and compliance.
1. Member is logged in and Identity (KYC) is Verified.
2. Member is not currently exceeding the maximum allowed active groups.
The Member inputs the group details:
validates that the proposed amount and rules stay within the
reviews the request details via the Admin Dashboard.
, making it visible in the public "Available
reviews the request and finds the terms violate university policy or appear predatory
notifies the Member and sets the group status to
Alternative Flow B: Creation Limit Reached:
detects that the user has already exceeded the maximum number of active groups allowed for a single member.
group is created and open for member enrollment.
Allows a student to browse and enroll in an existing sav
ings cycle that has open slots.
Member selects "Join" on a specific group.
The Member selects a specific group to view its terms (Amount, Rotation, etc.).
verifies that the group has available slots (Current Members < Max Members).
checks if the user is already a participant in that specific group.
adds the Member to the participant list for the group.
the group's visibility if it becomes full
disables the "Join" button and displays a "Group Full" notice
The member is successfully enrolled in the cycle and can begin contributing
The primary financial interaction where a member pays their scheduled contribution for the current round.
payment is due for the current round.
The Member selects their preferred payment method (e.g.,
redirects the user to the secure Payment Gateway interface (or simulates the USSD pipeline).
records the transaction in the digital ledger (Date, Amount,
updates the user’s status for the current round to
Alternative Flow A: Payment Gateway Failure
The user authorizes the payment, but the
notifies the user: "Transaction failed. Please check your balance or try again."
No changes are made to the group ledger.
The contribution is recorded as a successful transaction and added to the group fund
Provides transparency by allowing members to track the progress of their savings group.
The member is logged in and is a participant in the specific
queries the database for the current round's data.
for previous rounds in the current cycle.
and the list of members who have already paid for the current round.
The user is updated on the current state of their
The system automatically executes a fair, random selection algorithm to choose a winner once all required contributions for the round are confirmed.
The system detection logic confirms that the number of "Verified Payments" equals the "Maximum Members" for the current round.
participating members have successfully completed their
's logic detects that the number of "Verified Payments" matches the "Max Members" for the round.
locks the current round to prevent any late modifications.
filters the participant list to identify members who
algorithm to select one winner from the eligible pool.
status to the selected member and calculates the total payout.
updates the group ledger and creates a
to all group members announcing the winner.
The scheduled draw time arrives, but the
detects one or more members have not paid.
The draw remains pending until all ledger entries for the round are reconciled.
A winner is officially recorded, and the funds are moved to a "Ready for Withdrawal" state for that specific user.
Provides an audit trail for the user's personal financial activity
The user must be logged in and have an active account
retrieves all credit and debit entries associated with the user’s ID.
The Member can filter the history by a specific
Alternative Flow A: No Transaction History Found (Empty State)
queries the database using the user’s ID but finds zero records (e.g., a new user who has not yet joined or paid into an
displays a friendly "No Transactions Yet" message with a call-to-action to "Browse
Alternative Flow B: Data Retrieval Error (Persistence Layer Failure)
attempts to view their history, but the
is unable to connect to the
database due to a network timeout or server downtime.
displays an error notification: "Unable to load transaction history. Please check your connection and try again."
The user is provided with a transparent audit trail of their financial activity within the platform.
Admin verifies identity documents (KYC) or bans users for bad behavior.
The Administrator is logged into the Admin Dashboard with appropriate privileges
The Admin logs into the dashboard and selects
users who have recently signed up.
The Admin reviews the user's uploaded
to grant the user full access to start/join
updates the user status and sends a confirma
, prompting the user to re-upload documents
identifies a user reported for fraud and clicks
revokes the user's session and prevents future logins
The user's status is updated, and an audit trail of the admin action is logged
Allows the Admin to monitor active
groups to ensure they follow platform rules and to intervene in case of disputes or suspicious activity.
Admin selects "Active Groups" or receives a user report/flag regarding a specific
Admin is logged into the Admin Dashboard with authorized credentials.
1. Admin browses the list of currently active
2. Admin selects a group to view its health metrics (payment consistency, participant activity).
3. Admin reviews any system-generated flags or user-submitted reports.
4. Admin determines the group is operating safely and exits the view.
Alternative Flow A: Freezing a Group:
immediately halts all contributions and withdrawals for that cycle
Alternative Flow B: Dissolving a Group:
If a violation is irrecoverable, the
closes the cycle and triggers the refund logic for existing balances.
The group’s status is updated (Active, Frozen, or Terminated), and an audit log entry is created detailing the Admin’s action.
The Admin cannot change the contribution amount, modify the draw sequence, or manually withdraw funds for personal use.
An automated service that prompts members to make their contributions before the deadline.
he system-defined time (e.g., 24 hours before a payment deadline).
cycle is active and the payment deadline for the round is approaching or has passed
or SMS to the member's registered phone.
logs the delivery of the notification for the Admin's audit trail.
Members are notified of their upcoming financial obligation
Enables a winning member to transfer their accumulated savings to an external mobile money account.
User is identified as the "Winner" of a completed round.
The Winner confirms the amount and the destination account (e.g.,
initiates a payout request via the
The Gateway processes the transfer and returns a success confirmation.
updates the payout record status to
deducts the balance from the group's pooled fund and updates the ledger
Alternative Flow A: Insufficient Platform Liquidity
attempts to trigger the external transfer, but the gateway returns an error (e.g., service downtime).
and notifies the Admin to resolve the gateway link.
Funds are transferred to the user's external account, and the platform balance is updated
Allows Administrators to export system performance and financial data for university records.
Admin clicks "Export Report" in the admin dashboard
The Administrator is logged into the Admin Panel with reporting permissions
aggregates the required data from the primary database and logs.
The Admin downloads the file for external reporting.
A report file is created and provided to the administrator for download
This sequence diagram illustrates how a student registers and authenticates in
, including input validation, OTP verification, and credential-based access to the system.
This sequence diagram shows the lifecycle of an
group, from a member’s creation request through administrative approval to other members joining an active
This sequence diagram demonstrates how a user joins
This sequence diagram depicts the view
This sequence diagram depicts the contribution payment process, the system-triggered automated draw, and the sending of reminder and winner notifications.
This sequence diagram demonstrates how a winning member withdraws funds through the payment gateway and how administrators oversee system compliance and generate audit reports
The following activity diagram describes how a prospective member registers
The following activity diagram describes how member login to the
he following activity diagram describes how a member submits an
creation request, how an administrator reviews it, and how the system activates or rejects the
The following activity diagram illustrates how a member gets access to the payment
The following activity diagram illustrates how members get to see their
The following activity diagram illustrates how a member gets access to the payment history
The following activity diagram illustrates how a member joins an active
, makes a contribution, and how the system verifies and records the payment.
The following activity diagram shows how the system checks contribution completion, performs an automated draw, assigns a winner, and notifies members accordingly.

illustrates how the notification reminder process works

diagram reflects how the system pulls information from various parts of the database
The Analysis Level Class Diagram defines the static structure of the
system, identifying the key objects, their attributes, methods, and relationships.
The analysis-level class diagram is used to signify which are the fundamental entities of the system
and their association. We have an abstract User entity that represents all users of the system
that is then specialized to Member which members which are part of
and the Admin who manages validation/compliance. An
is raised by a Member and reviewed by an Admin to birth an
can include many compounds as members of the compounds library. An
group has several Round instances which can be viewed as contribution cycles resulting in a

winning Member. The transaction entity is used to model interactions of the financial system, and the notification entity would be used to model notification such as reminders or alerts. The above constituents determine the static structure of the system
This chapter presents the system design of the UNI-
application, which is developed to support university students in organizing and managing their savings using a digital
system. The system design phase converts the requirements found in previous chapters into a technical and structured blueprint that directs the application's development and implementation.
The design concentrates on how the system will work, how various parts will communicate with one another, and how users will engage with the program. Additionally, it ensures that t

he system is effective, safe, easy to use, and able to manage students' real-world usage. This chapter lowers development risks and guarantees that the finished system achieves its goals by precisely outlining the system architecture and design components.
4.2 Purpose of the System Design Document

tem Design Document (SDD) aims to give a thorough explanation of the system's construction and functionality. This document provides developers, project managers, and stakeholders with a clear and structured explanation of the system's technical structure.
The system design document specifically seeks to:

To Provide a Road map: It provides the development team with a clear blueprint for the system's construction, demonstrating how various components—such as the database and user interface—work together.
handles student money, this document defines the technical rules that keep the data safe and the draws fair.
To Guide Developers: It provides the "how-to" instructions for programmers so they can build the system exactly as it was planned.
To Save Time and Resources: By planning the system architecture now, we can find and fix potential technical problems before we start writing the code.
The scope of this System Design Document (SDD) encompasses the complete architectural and detailed design of the
platform. This design builds directly upon the functional and non-functional requirements defined during the requirement phase.
The design scope includes the following core modules:
User Authentication & Security: Detailed design of the authentication system and integration with local KYC (Know Your Customer) standards.
Management: Design of the logic for creating
groups, calculating contribution cycles, and managing payout rotations.
Automated Draw System: The technical specification for a fair, verifiable, and automated selection process for winners.
Payment Integration Layer: Architectural design for interfacing with Ethiopian payment gateways (e.g.,
Notification & Reporting: Design of the background services for SMS/Push reminders and real-time ledger generation for users.
The architectural design establishes a structured solution that addresses the technical and operational requirements of
, with a focus on optimizing performance, security, and maintainability.
The architecture is primarily shaped by the financial characteristics of the application and the specific needs of the student user base.
The system ensures complete protection of data. All remote access is subject to user
identification (phone number) and password control. Secure communication (HTTPS) is mandatory to protect transaction data.
The system must be available 24/7 to allow students to check their status or make payments at any time.
The application must function effectively even in low-bandwidth network environments common on campus.
The client portion resides on Android mobile devices, while the server portion operates on a cloud-hosted environment (
system is comprised of three main layers: The

Architecture pattern, separating concerns into Presentation, Business Logic, and Data Persistence. This separation is critical for financial security, ensuring that the user interface never interacts directly with the database or the core financial ledger.
This layer resides on the user's Android device. It is responsible for input validation and rendering data, but it contains no business rules.
Captures user credentials and payment requests.
Displays the active state of the
: Receives background signals for draw results and payment reminders.
This is the core of the system, residing on the cloud server. It processes all requests and enforces the rules of the
. It is subdivided into three critical engines:
Manages the state of every group (Pending, Active, Locked).
Enforces the "Round" logic, ensuring a new round cannot start until the previous one is reconciled.
Scheduler Service: Triggers the automated draw once all payments for a cycle are verified.
B. Payment Processing Engine (Virtual Account Manager):
This component manages the integration with Chapa.
To ensure funds are never mixed between groups, the system utilizes a Virtual Account concept. When an
group is created, the system generates a unique Payment Reference Key specific to that group.
When a member contributes, the payment is tagged with this Reference Key. The Payment Engine listens for Chapa
to confirm receipt and automatically routes the credit to the specific Group Ledger, ensuring 100% accurate reconciliation without manual intervention.
This logic ensures that Group A's funds can never be logically accessed by Group B, mimicking a dedicated bank account for each group.
Contains the Random Number Generator (RNG) logic.
Enforces "Win-Once" rules to ensure a member cannot win twice in the same cycle until the cycle resets.
This layer manages the storage and retrieval of data while maintaining Referential Integrity.

: The database is designed so that Transaction records are append-only. Once a payment is verified via the Virtual Account logic, it cannot be deleted or altered, providing a secure audit trail for all student funds.
The process view describes the tasks and threads involved in the system’s execution. For
, several key processes run concurrently to ensure smooth operation.
Manages user login and session security using tokens.
table and ensures the account is not locked.
Initiated when a member submits a payment.
This process communicates with the external Payment Gateway (Chapa) or verifies a manual entry.
This is a conditional process. It monitors the state of an active round.
The system runs the random selection algorithm, assigns a
An asynchronous thread that sends SMS or Push Notifications for payment reminders or win announcements.
To ensure transparent and automated contribution tracking, the system utilizes
Upon the creation and approval of a new
group, the system makes an API call to Chapa to generate a unique
This virtual account remains active for the entire duration of the
lifecycle (across all rounds). It serves as the digital "pot" for the group.
When a member transfers their contribution to this Group Virtual Account, Chapa sends a
normalizes the incoming phone number format (converting +251 to 09)
and matches this number against the group's member list to automatically mark the specific member as 'Paid' for the current round.
cycle is completed or dissolved, the system marks the Virtual Account as inactive in the database, preventing future deposits.
The deployment view describes the physical nodes and platform configuration
Provides the interface for students to join groups and view financial history.
Stores all data, runs the winner selection logic, and manages API requests.
Processes real-money transfers and returns transaction status to the
The Android Client connects via the Internet (HTTPS) to the Cloud Server. The Cloud Server maintains a secure connection to the Database and initiates API calls to the External Payment Gateway.
The chosen software architecture supports the key sizing and timing requirements
) is designed to support concurrent connections, suitable for the projected user base of university students.
The system is optimized to complete critical transactions (like joining a group or recording a payment) rapidly, adhering to the requirement of network optimization for poor connections
The client application is lightweight, requiring minimum RAM (2GB) and storage (100MB) on the student's device
Database Design encompasses a set of processes which enable the design, development, implementation, and management of the
enterprise data management systems. The correctly designed
be more maintainable, improve the consistency of the data, and be more cost-effective in terms of storage. Specifically within the
system, it has been determined which pieces of information are related, such as Users,
Groups, Rounds, and Transactions, and what information must be collected to provide financial integrity.
The primary task of database design in this project is to create logical and physical design models of the centralized database. The design of a logical

database concentrates on data requirements, such as the student and payment relationship. The physical database design concentrates on the actual implementation of storing data in a way that is understood and implemented by the database (in this context,
), ensuring that data such as financial information and lottery outcomes is independent of physical constraints and other personal identifying information
The logical database design identifies the entities, attributes, primary keys, and relationships required to support the
system requirements, the following main entities were identified:
Each table is designed to store atomic data and to support system operations such as creating
, contributing money, receiving payouts, applying penalties, and generating reports.
Stores student registration details, authentication credentials, and KYC status.
Full legal name of the student
URL path to the uploaded ID image
Stores the configuration for each saving group (
Links to the User who started the group
The unique virtual account number assigned to this group
The date the first cycle begins
A junction table establishing the Many-to-Many relationship between Users and Groups.
Manages the specific cycles within a group (e.g., Week 1, Week 2) and tracks the winner.
Unique identifier for the specific round
The group this round belongs to
The User ID selected by RNG (
Records all financial movements (Contributions and Payouts).
The user making or receiving payment
The specific round the payment is for
system, the ER diagram depicts the relationship of students to groups and each group producing rounds, as well as the association of transactions to students and rounds.
: One-to-Many (A user can create many groups).
One-to-Many (A user can belong to many groups).
to Rounds: One-to-Many (A group has multiple rounds/cycles
Round to Transactions: One-to-Many (a round has multiple contribution transactions).
Relational mapping is the process of converting the conceptual ent

ities identified in the ER model into actual database tables (relations) while establishing the connections between them using Primary Keys (PK) and Foreign Keys (FK). This step bridges the gap between the logical design and the physical implementation in
system, the mapping ensures referential integrity. For instance, a Transaction cannot exist without a valid User and Round. Similarly, the Many-to-Many relationship between Users and Groups is mapped
using an intermediate junction table called
To ensure data integrity, minimize redundancy, and prevent update anomalies, the
database schema was developed through a structured normalization process. The design progressed systematically from
-related data was conceptually represented in a flat structure (similar to a spreadsheet). This design resulted in excessive redundancy—for
were repeated for every transaction row.
Update Anomaly: If the group’s virtual account changes, every transaction record must be updated.
Redundancy: Member details (e.g., phone number) are repeated for every transaction.
attribute depends on the Round, not on individual transactions.
is introduced to uniquely identify each record.
All attributes are ensured to contain
Although atomicity is achieved, redundancy and dependency issues still remain.
by separating attributes that do not depend on the entire primary key.
Even though the Transactions table now satisfies
, not by the individual Transaction. As a result, if all transaction records
for a specific round (e.g., Round 1) are deleted, the information about who won that round is also lost. This violates normalization principles and motivates further decomposition in
The Winner attribute is logically determined by the Round, not by individual transactions. Therefore, round-related data is extracted into a separate table.
To resolve this issue, a separate Rounds table was introduced.
Stores group configuration and shared virtual account details.
Stores round-level data, including winners and cycle status.
Stores payment history linked to users and rounds.
This normalized schema ensures that updates—such as user profile changes or group configuration updates—occur in a single location, thereby fully satisfying
requirements and improving data integrity, consistency, and maintainability.
was to address a limitation of the conventional, manually operated
system in a university setting. By making use of contemporary web and mobile technology,
was able to design an automated finance system that delivers automated operations of group creation, members' registration, and contributions.
This system addresses effectively long-standing challenges

associated with the conventional system, such as a lack of transparency, restrictions in terms of geographical location, and human influence in conducting lottery. With a fair lottery system in place through an algorithm, along with statuses in real time,
inculcates a culture of trust and fiscal responsibility in students and staff.

In addition to that, it shows that it’s possible to develop and re-engineer financial culture practices for modern-day needs and necessities. The application of an efficient technical stack—such as React Native and
—means that not only is it fully functional and operational but it’s also safe and scalable for dealing with sensitive user information. Lastly,
becomes an essential platform for financial liberation by ensuring students
better use of their finances while enhancing digital inclusion on campus.
: Although the current implementation is done in a sandbox, the future versions could be developed with the integration of the real local payment systems such as
, CBE Birr, or Chapa, enabling live financial operations.
The design of the system is quite flexible and can be applicable on different scales, i.e., not only on one university. In the future versions of
, different campuses of universities can also have
: The application should also incorporate advanced security mechanisms, including consumer authentication through fingerprint or facial recognition, in addition to security auditing.
, G. (2015). Informal financial institutions and saving culture in Ethiopia. International Journal of Social Economics, 42(10), 861–875.
, D. (2004). Rotating savings and credit associations (
) in Ethiopia: Characteristics and economic role. Ethiopian Journal of Economics, 13(1), 1–20.
The purpose of this interview was to understand how traditional
systems operate in real-life settings, identify challenges faced by
collectors, and gather requirements for designing a student-focused digital
Responses recorded manually during the interview
Appendix B: Summary of Interview Results (Traditional
participated in the interview. All participants had practical experience managing one or more
groups and were responsible for member coordination, payment collection, and record keeping.
B.2 Summary of Key Interview Findings
The interview data revealed consistent operational patterns and challenges across all participants. The summarized findings are presented below.
delay payment in each contribution cycle
are willing to use a mobile app
The interviews highlighted several critical challenges in traditional
Manual payment verification is time-consuming and unreliable
Paper-based record keeping leads to data loss and errors
Appendix C: Student Google Form Survey Questionnaire
The Google Form survey was conducted among university students to understand their saving habits, familiarity with
, and their willingness to adopt a digital
Potential users of a student-focused digital
Responses exported to Microsoft Excel for analysis
The student survey covered the following areas:
Appendix D: Summary of Student Survey Results (Google Form)
who are either currently participating in
systems or interested in saving through
Mostly 2nd–4th year students from various departments
Payment failure, trust, and data security
Link for the data collected from the 71 students
The survey results indicate that most students are familiar with
practices and show strong interest in a digital alternative. However, successful adoption of the
reliable payments, strong security, and transparent system features
Appendix E: Combined Insights from Interviews and Student Survey
study confirms that both organizers and students face similar challenges related to:
The findings from both data sources informed the design of the
No personal identifying information was collected
Data was used solely for academic purposes
